h: You've been doing it all along
b: Unit 0 · Start here What is machine learning? You already know how to do this. You've just never called it that. You've been doing it all along Think about how you learned to guess whether it'll rain from the look of the sky. Nobody gave you a rulebook. You saw a lot of skies, noticed what followed, and your guesses got better. The "rule" in your head — dark and heavy in the west means bring an umbrella — was never written down. It grew out of examples. That is the whole trick. Ordinary programming is writing the rule down : a person types "IF the sky is dark THEN predict rain," and the computer obeys. Machine learning flips it: we show the computer lots of examples with known outcomes, and it works out a rule of its own — by adjusting its internal settings until its guesses match the examples well. The thing being adjusted is called a model : a guessing machine with tunable settings. Training is the adjustment process. That's the vocabulary — everything else on this site is detail, and each detail gets its own page. The two big families Most of what you'll meet falls into one of two situations: Learning with an answer key — every example comes with the right answer attached: this email was spam, this house did sell for $400k. The computer practices and gets graded. This is called supervised learning , and it covers two kinds of question: predict a number ( regression ), or predict a category ( classification ). Learning without an answer key — just a pile of data, and the job is to find structure in it: which customers behave alike? This is unsupervised learning . Modern AI — chatbots, image generators — is built on the same foundations, with more layers of cleverness stacked on top. By Unit 6 you'll be able to see the line from "fit a straight line through dots" to "write me a poem." What ML is not A model is not smart, and it doesn't "understand." It's a settings-tuning process that makes a guessing machine agree with past examples. That has two practical consequ

h: Statistics: a pile of numbers, summarized in two
The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 1 · Math foundations Probability & statistics: the math of "usually" Models guess. Probability is the honest language for guessing — and statistics is how we summarize a pile of examples into a few useful numbers. Before you start How to read math — we use μ and σ from the Greek letter table (both re-explained here). Probability: what 70% actually means "70% chance of rain" doesn't mean it will 70%-rain. It means: out of many days exactly like this one, about 70 in 100 turn rainy. Probability is long-run bookkeeping — a single event just happens or doesn't; the number describes the pattern across many repeats. ML speaks this language constantly. A spam filter doesn't say "spam"; it says "0.93" — of emails that look like this, about 93 in 100 turn out to be spam . Someone (you, in Unit 2's metrics page) then chooses where to draw the line. Try it: watch "usually" emerge A fair coin is 50% heads — but watch how messy the road to 50% is. Flip once, then a hundred times, then ten thousand: Flip once Flip 100 Flip 10,000 Reset Flips: 0 · heads: 0 · share of heads so far: – Notice: after 10 flips, 70% heads is unremarkable. After 10,000, it would be astonishing. Small samples lie — the single most practical lesson in this unit. (The x-axis is stretched so you can see the wild early wobbles.) Statistics: a pile of numbers, summarized in two Given a thousand measurements, two numbers tell most of the story: The mean (μ) — the plain average. Where the pile is centered. The standard deviation (σ) — the typical distance from that center. Are values huddled tight or scattered wide? The shape of the pile is called a distribution . One shape appears everywhere — measurement errors, heights, averages of anything: the bell curve, or normal distribution . Its handy rule of thumb: about 68% of values fall within one σ of the mean, about 95% within two σ. The math (open as much as you like) The idea in words Mean: add everything, divide by the count. Standard deviation: measure e

h: Try it: read an equation piece by piece
The Greek letters you'll actually meet
b: Unit 1 · Math foundations How to read math Math notation is not knowledge — it's an alphabet. This page teaches you to sound it out, so a wall of symbols becomes a sentence you can read slowly. Before you start Nothing. This page exists so that every other page needs less courage. The secret: equations are sentences Every equation is a sentence with a verb in the middle. The verb is usually = , and it reads "is". Left of it: the thing being defined. Right of it: the recipe for it. So ŷ = wx + b is just the sentence "the prediction is the input times a weight, plus an offset." Once you spot the verb, you read the rest a piece at a time — never all at once. The three habits that make math readable: Find the verb first (=, ≈, ←) and split the sentence there. Sound out symbols by name — "theta", "sum of", "y-hat". A symbol you can say stops being scary. Letters are just labeled boxes. There's nothing special about x — it's a box someone labeled tersely. Mentally rename it: x is "the temperature", w is "how much it matters". Try it: read an equation piece by piece This is the formula for a plain old average — something you already understand. Step through it and watch familiar knowledge put on fancy clothes: \mu = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i The Greek letters you'll actually meet ML uses maybe a dozen Greek letters, and each has a habitual job. You don't need to memorize this table — it's here to come back to, and every page names its symbols anyway. h: The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 1 · Math foundations Slopes & gradients: the nudge question All of calculus that ML needs fits in one question: "if I nudge this a tiny bit, what happens to that?" Before you start Just How to read math . No calculus memories required — we're rebuilding the one piece ML uses, not the whole course. The idea Stand on a hillside. Without opening your eyes you can answer two questions: which way is downhill, and how steep is it. That local, eyes-closed knowledge — steepness right where I'm standing — is what a derivative is. On a chart: pick a point on a curve and zoom way in. Every curve looks straight up close. The steepness of that little straight piece is the derivative at that point. Positive: the curve is rising there. Negative: falling. Zero: flat — you're standing in a valley bottom or on a hilltop. Try it: feel the slope Drag the ball along the hill and watch the dashed line — that's the curve's steepness at the ball, updated live. Then find a spot, press Release the ball , and watch it roll downhill and settle in a valley. Release the ball Reset Steepness here: – · Try releasing the ball from the far left, then from the middle bump. Notice it doesn't always find the deepest valley — just the nearest one downhill. Remember that; it becomes a real issue when training big models. You just watched gradient descent The rolling ball is not a metaphor we bolted on — it's literally the algorithm. In the linear regression demo , "let the computer fit it" does exactly what the ball does: check the slope where you stand, step downhill, repeat. The hill there is the loss (the model's grade) and the ball's position is the model's settings. One upgrade and you have the real thing: real models have many settings, not one. The hill becomes a landscape with many directions, and the slope becomes a list of slopes — one per setting: "nudging w does this, nudging b does that." A list of numbers is a vector (last page!), and this particular vector has a name: the gradient . I

h: The one operation to actually learn: the dot product
The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 1 · Math foundations Vectors & matrices: lists and tables of numbers The two container types all of ML is built from — and the one operation between them that you'll see a thousand times. Before you start How to read math helps but isn't required. The idea A vector is just a list of numbers — that's the whole definition. What makes it useful is a second way of seeing the same list: as an arrow . The list [3, 1] is also "the arrow you get by walking 3 steps east and 1 step north." One object, two views: a row of boxes when you're storing data, an arrow when you're thinking about direction and similarity. Why ML cares: every data point is a vector. A house is [size, bedrooms, age]. An email becomes a (very long) list of numbers. The moment data becomes vectors, geometry starts answering questions — "which emails are near this one?" becomes a distance between arrows. A matrix is the next container up: a table of numbers, or equally, a stack of vectors — one row per data point. A spreadsheet of 1,000 houses × 3 facts is a 1000×3 matrix. That's all "matrix" means. The one operation to actually learn: the dot product Take two lists of the same length. Multiply them position by position, add the results up: one number out. That's the dot product . It has a beautiful secret: that number measures agreement between directions . Arrows pointing the same way → big positive number. Opposite ways → negative. At right angles → zero, "nothing to do with each other." Try it: drag the arrows Drag the tips of the two arrows and watch the lists and the dot product react. Try to make the dot product exactly zero — notice what the arrows look like when you succeed. a = [3.0, 1.0] · b = [1.0, 2.5] → dot product: – The dot product is how a model asks "how much does this input look like the pattern I'm tuned for?" — you'll see it inside every neural network layer. The math (open as much as you like) The idea in words Multiply matching positions, add it all up. For [3, 1] · [1, 2.5]: 3×

h: Try it: one dataset, two views
Where models actually win or lose
b: Unit 2 · Core concepts Data & features: rows, columns, and dots Every dataset is a notebook: one row per thing, one column per fact about it. Learn to see the notebook and the chart as the same object. Before you start Vectors & matrices helps — a row of the notebook is a vector. The idea Back to the ice cream stand. Your notebook has one row per day, with three columns: the temperature, whether it was a weekend, and how much you sold. In ML vocabulary: Each row is a data point — one example. The columns you know in advance (temperature, weekend?) are features — what the model predicts from . The column you want to predict (sales) is the label — the answer key. That's it. "Dataset" = notebook. "Features" = the known columns. "Label" = the answer column. The whole of supervised learning is: use the filled-in rows to predict the answer column for rows that don't have one yet. Try it: one dataset, two views Below, the same eight days as a notebook and as a chart. Hover a row and watch its dot light up; hover a dot and find its row. A data point isn't shown as a dot — it is the dot, and the row, at once. Day Temperature (feature) Weekend? (feature) Sales € (label) Mon 14° no 38 Tue 17° no 52 Wed 16° no 47 Thu 21° no 74 Fri 24° no 90 Sat 26° yes 118 Sun 23° yes 98 Mon 12° no 31 The chart only shows two of the three columns (temperature vs. sales). The weekend column is a third direction the flat screen can't draw — with 100 features, a data point is a dot in 100-dimensional space. Nobody can picture that; the trick is that the math (dot products, distances) works the same in 100 dimensions as in 2. You never need to picture it. Where models actually win or lose A dull truth with expensive consequences: most real ML work is work on the data, not the model. Things practitioners check before touching any algorithm: Is the label honest? If "sales" was written down from memory a week later, the answer key itself is noisy. Do the features carry the signal? h: Try it: you draw the line
The two questions, in plain words
b: Unit 2 · Core concepts Judging classifiers: four ways to be right or wrong "95% accurate" can describe a great model or a useless one. This page gives you the two questions that expose the difference. Before you start Probability — models score with numbers like 0.93, and someone must choose where to draw the line. On this page, that someone is you. The idea A spam filter's verdict on one email lands in exactly one of four boxes: Spam, flagged as spam — caught it. (A true positive .) Real email, left alone — correct pass. (A true negative .) Real email, flagged as spam — a false alarm : your friend's message dies in the junk folder. Spam, left alone — a miss : junk in your inbox. Here's the part every practitioner has tattooed somewhere: the two mistakes cost different amounts, and which one costs more depends entirely on the problem. Junk in the inbox is annoying; a real contract offer silently junked is a disaster. For a cancer screen, the costs reverse-and-multiply. No single number can weigh both mistakes for you — which is why "accuracy" alone is never enough. Try it: you draw the line The model has scored 100 emails from "looks fine" (left) to "looks spammy" (right). Real emails sit in the top band, actual spam in the bottom band; the tinted zone is what gets flagged. Wrongly-handled emails glow red. Drag the cutoff: Where to draw the line (the threshold ) 0.50 flagged as spam left alone actually spam caught – missed (junk in inbox) – actually real false alarm (lost email!) – correct pass – Accuracy (share handled correctly): – · precision (of the flagged, how many really were spam): – · recall (of the actual spam, how much we caught): – Slide right: false alarms vanish (precision rises) but spam slips through (recall falls). Slide left: the reverse. You cannot max out both — the overlap zone in the middle is emails the model genuinely can't tell apart, and no threshold fixes that. Only a better model shrinks the overlap. The two questions, in plain words Prec

h: Try it: turn the bendiness dial
b: Unit 2 · Core concepts Overfitting: memorizing instead of learning The central occupational disease of machine learning. This page hands you the dial that causes it, so you can feel exactly where learning tips into memorizing. Before you start Training vs. testing — this page leans entirely on the train/test split. The idea Give a model more flexibility and it fits the training data better. Always. More bend, better practice grade — nothing stops that trend, ever. But somewhere along the way, the extra bend stops capturing the pattern and starts wrapping around the noise : the accidents of which particular dots landed where. From that point on, the test grade turns around and gets worse while the training grade keeps improving. That's overfitting . Its mirror image — a model too stiff to catch the real pattern at all, bad grades everywhere — is underfitting . Between them sits a sweet spot, and finding it is much of the day-to-day craft of ML. Try it: turn the bendiness dial The dial below controls how bendy the fitted curve may be (technically the polynomial degree — how many times the curve is allowed to change direction). Sweep it slowly from 1 to 12 and watch both grades: How bendy the curve may be 1 Re-deal the split Grade on practice dots (train): – · grade on hidden dots (test): – Verdict: Watch the train grade only ever improve as you add bend — while the test grade improves, bottoms out around 3–5, then climbs. That U-shape is the most important curve in applied ML. At degree 12, look at the curve's wild swings between dots: those lurches ARE memorized noise. What to do about it Overfitting has standard treatments, all with the same flavor — make memorizing harder than learning: More data. The cure that always works when you can afford it: noise averages out, patterns don't. Twice the dots makes the bendy curve's tricks visibly fail on its own training set. A simpler model. Turn the dial down. Fewer knobs, less capacity to memorize. Regularization . Keep th

h: Try it: same dots, two grades
b: Unit 2 · Core concepts Training vs. testing: the honesty rule Never grade a student on the exact questions they practiced. The same courtesy applies to models — and forgetting it is the most common way ML projects fool their own creators. Before you start Data & features , and it helps to have seen a model get graded — the linear regression demo if you're curious, though this page stands alone. The idea A student who memorized the answer sheet aces the practice exam. Do they know the subject? You find out with one move: new questions. ML copies that move exactly. Before training, split the notebook: The training set (typically ~70–80% of rows) — the model practices on these; its settings are tuned to fit them. The test set — locked in a drawer. The model never sees these during training. At the end, they're the real exam. The grade on the training set tells you how well the model fit what it saw . The grade on the test set estimates what actually matters: how it will do out in the world, on data nobody has labels for yet. When the two grades disagree badly, the model memorized instead of learned — and only the split lets you catch it. Doing well on new data has a name: generalization . It is the entire point. Try it: same dots, two grades Filled dots are the training set; hollow red dots are the test set, hidden during fitting. Fit a simple line, then a very bendy curve, and watch the two grades tell different stories: Fit a simple line Fit a very bendy curve Re-deal the split Grade on practice dots (train): – · grade on hidden dots (test): – Verdict: The bendy curve gets a better practice grade than the line and a worse exam grade — it bent itself around noise. Also press "re-deal" a few times: the exact numbers wobble depending on which dots landed in which pile. Grades from small test sets are themselves "small samples" — remember the coin flips. The rules of the drawer The test set only works if it stays untouched. Three practical rules: Split first, look later.

h: Try it: grow a tree, then hire a forest
Why the mob beats the expert
b: Unit 3 · Classical ML Decision trees & forests: twenty questions, then a crowd of them A model you can read like a flowchart — and the trick that turns a mob of unreliable flowcharts into one of the most reliable tools in ML. Before you start Overfitting — trees are the most visual overfitters in all of ML, and this page leans on that. The idea Play twenty questions with the data: "Is the humidity under 4.1? If yes — is the temperature under 2.9? If yes — it's class A." A decision tree is exactly that flowchart, grown automatically: at each step the algorithm tries every possible yes/no question about every feature and keeps the one that best unmixes the classes — the split after which each side is as single-colored as possible. Then it repeats inside each side, until the groups are pure or a depth limit says stop. Because every question is about one feature at a time ("is x ≤ 4.1?"), a tree's map of the world is all straight fences at right angles — little boxes. And because it can keep asking questions, a deep tree can build a private box around every single dot . You know the name for that. Try it: grow a tree, then hire a forest The depth dial limits how many questions deep the flowchart may go. The actual questions the tree learned are printed below the chart — this is the rare model you can fully read: How many questions deep 3 One tree Plant a forest (25 trees) – · Crank depth to 10 and find the absurd little boxes built around single stray dots. Then press "plant a forest": 25 deep trees, each trained on a slightly different random re-sample of the dots, voting together. Each tree still memorizes noise — but they memorize different noise, and the vote averages it away. Deep color = near-unanimous; pale = the trees disagree. Why the mob beats the expert That button is the biggest idea on this page, worth saying carefully. A single deep tree is a jumpy model — re-deal the data and it draws a different map (high variance, from the overfitting page). The random

h: Try it: run the dance yourself
The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Classical ML k-means: when nobody gives you the answers Your first model with no answer key. Just dots, a hunch about how many groups there are, and a two-step dance that sorts them out. Before you start kNN — same notion of distance, opposite situation: there we had labels and asked neighbors; here nobody has a label at all. The idea A shop owner has data on a thousand customers but no categories — nobody labeled anyone "bargain hunter" or "big spender". The question isn't "predict the label" (there are none); it's " do these customers form natural groups? " Finding groups in unlabeled data is called clustering , and k-means is its classic recipe. You supply one hunch: k, the number of groups to look for. The algorithm places k guess-centers, then repeats two steps that you can fully understand in one sentence each: Color the dots: every dot joins its nearest center. Move the centers: every center relocates to the middle of the dots that joined it (that middle is its centroid — just the mean, feature by feature). Repeat until no dot wants to switch. That's the entire algorithm — no gradients, no loss curves, just musical chairs settling down. Try it: run the dance yourself The dots are unlabeled (gray). The ✕ marks are the guess-centers, placed randomly. Step through the dance and watch order appear: How many groups to look for (k) 3 Step 1: color the dots Run until settled Restart with new random centers Next up: · total scatter around centers: – (lower = tighter groups) Two experiments worth running: (1) Restart several times at k=3 — different random starts can settle into different answers; k-means only promises a resting point, not the best one (the rolling ball's nearest-valley problem, again — real toolkits just run it many times and keep the tightest result). (2) Set k=5 on this clearly-3-group data — k-means will dutifully shatter real groups to satisfy your bad hunch. The algorithm never questions k; choosing it is your job. The math (open as muc

h: The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Classical ML k-nearest neighbors: just ask the locals The simplest classifier that actually works — no training, no equations, no boundary. It remembers everything and, when asked, polls the neighborhood. Before you start Vectors — "nearest" means distance, and distance between data points is distance between vectors. The idea You find an unfamiliar animal photo. Your instinct: compare it to animals you know — it most resembles a few cats you've seen, so, probably a cat. That entire instinct is the algorithm. k-nearest neighbors (kNN) classifies a new point by finding the k most similar known examples and letting them vote. Note what's missing: there is no training step. The "model" is the dataset, kept whole. All the work happens at question time — measure the distance to every stored example, take the k closest, count the votes. Slow to answer, instant to "train", and surprisingly strong: with enough data, the locals usually know. Try it: poll the neighborhood Three classes of pets, plotted by two features. Move your mouse — the dashed lines point to the k nearest known animals, and their vote follows your cursor. Then paint the whole map and see the territory each class controls: How many neighbors get a vote (k) 3 Paint the whole map The vote: move the mouse over the chart Paint the map at k=1, then at k=15. At k=1, single stray dots carve their own little islands — that's overfitting, in its purest visible form. Big k smooths the borders but starts steamrolling small legitimate regions. Yes: k is a bendiness dial, and you've already learned how to pick one (a validation set). The math (open as much as you like) The idea in words "Similar" = "nearby," and nearby is measured with the straight-line distance you learned as Pythagoras: square the gap in each feature, add, square-root. Then: sort by distance, keep k, majority wins. Show the math d(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (a_i - b_i)^2} In plain English The distance between two data poi

h: Try it: fit the line yourself
The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Classical ML Linear regression: teaching a line to make predictions The simplest machine that learns — and the one that quietly contains most of the big ideas in machine learning. Before you start You only need to be comfortable reading a dot chart (a scatter plot). Any math that appears below is optional, collapsible, and explained symbol by symbol. The idea Imagine you run an ice cream stand. You've noticed that hotter days mean more sales, and you've kept notes: each day is one dot on a chart — temperature across, sales up. Now you'd like a rule of thumb : "for any temperature, roughly how much will I sell?" The simplest possible rule is a straight line drawn through your dots. To predict a day's sales, find the temperature on the line and read off the answer. That's linear regression . The only question is: which line? Some lines clearly summarize the dots better than others. Machine learning's answer: give each candidate line a grade — how far off its predictions are from the real answers — and find the line with the best grade. That grade is called the loss (lower is better, like golf). Try it: fit the line yourself The dots below follow a hidden pattern plus some randomness. Use the two sliders to move the line until its grade is as low as you can get it. Then press "Let the computer fit it" and watch it do the same thing — just faster. How steep the line is (the weight, w) 0.20 Where the line starts (the bias, b) 5.00 Let the computer fit it Show the mistakes (residuals) Reset Your line's grade (average squared mistake): – · best possible for these dots: – Tip: click anywhere on the chart to add your own dot and see how it drags the best line around. One weird dot far from the others (an outlier ) has a surprisingly big effect — that's the squaring at work. How the computer "learns" the line When you pressed the button, the computer didn't try every possible line. It did something smarter, and this three-step loop is the heart of most machine learni

h: Try it: watch it find the boundary
The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Classical ML Logistic regression: a line that gives odds Take linear regression, bend its output into a probability, and you have the workhorse of yes/no prediction — still, decades on, the first model every practitioner tries. Before you start Linear regression (this page reuses its recipe) and judging classifiers (this model outputs exactly the kind of scores you thresholded there). The idea Suppose you're predicting a yes/no: will this customer buy ice cream? The linear recipe — multiply each feature by a weight, add up — still produces a useful number: strongly positive means "very buy-ish", strongly negative "very not". But it ranges over all numbers, and a probability must live between 0 and 1. So we pass the score through a squashing function: very negative scores squash toward 0, very positive toward 1, and a score of exactly zero lands at 50/50. The squasher is called the sigmoid (it's S-shaped), and the whole assembly is logistic regression . Despite the name, it's a classifier — the "regression" is a historical fossil. Geometrically, the model draws one straight line through feature-space: the 50/50 line, its decision boundary . Everything on one side leans yes, the other no — with confidence growing the farther you go from the line. Try it: watch it find the boundary Red dots are one class, blue the other. The background is the model's current opinion — deeper color means more confident, the pale ribbon is the 50/50 boundary. The model starts knowing nothing (everything 50/50). Press train and watch gradient descent swing the boundary into place: Train (watch it learn) Click adds: blue Click adds: red Reset Grade (log loss): – · dots currently on the right side: – After training, click a few blue dots deep inside red territory and retrain. The boundary shifts, but it stays a straight line — logistic regression cannot bend. h: Try it: one training step, in slow motion
b: Unit 4 · Neural networks Backpropagation: blame, passed backwards The algorithm that trains every neural network on Earth is a bookkeeping trick: compute forward, then walk the recipe in reverse, handing each step its share of the blame. Before you start Slopes & gradients (the nudge question) — backprop is that question, answered wholesale. The idea Training needs to know: "if I nudge this weight, what happens to the loss?" — for every one of possibly billions of weights. Measuring each by actually nudging it would take one full network-run per weight. Hopeless. Backpropagation gets every answer in just two passes. The insight: a network is a recipe of small steps (multiply, add, bend), and each step knows its own local slope. The chain rule — the only calculus fact backprop needs — says nudge-effects along a chain multiply : if a nudge to w moves z by 2×, and a nudge to z moves the loss by 3×, then w moves the loss by 6×. So: run the recipe forward and save every intermediate value; then walk backward from the loss, multiplying local slopes as you go. Arriving at each weight, the accumulated product IS its gradient. Two passes, all answers. Try it: one training step, in slow motion One neuron, one example (input 2, truth "yes"), currently making a bad prediction. Step through the forward pass (values, left to right), then the backward pass (blame, right to left — red numbers). Then apply the nudge and run it again: ← Back Next step → Apply the nudge Start over Do the full cycle three or four times — forward, backward, nudge — and watch the loss trail shrink toward zero. Notice x's box never shows a red number we act on: we can't nudge the data, only the knobs. (The numbers here are computed by this site's real autodiff engine — the same 100 lines you'll meet below.) The same trick, at scale Everything you just stepped through scales without changing character: A billion-parameter network is still a recipe of multiply/add/bend steps — just a very long one. h: Try it: build a function out of neurons
b: Unit 4 · Neural networks Stacking neurons: where the bends come from One neuron draws one bent line. A layer of them, summed, can draw bumps, steps, and wiggles — and with enough neurons, any shape at all. Before you start The neuron . This page is one idea, built with your own hands. The idea Wire neurons in layers: every input feeds every neuron in the first layer (a hidden layer — "hidden" only means "between input and output", nothing spookier), those outputs feed the next layer, and so on to the final answer. This assembly is a multi-layer perceptron ( MLP ) — the plain bread-and-butter neural network. The question that matters: where does the expressive power come from? The demo below lets you feel the answer. A single ReLU neuron contributes a humble bent ramp: flat, then a slope, one kink. But sums of ramps make shapes . Two opposing ramps make a plateau. Three make a bump. More make wiggles, staircases, anything — the kinks are little joints, and with enough joints a chain of straight pieces can trace any curve. That, informally, is the famous universal approximation property. Try it: build a function out of neurons Three ReLU neurons (thin colored lines) and their sum (thick line). Each neuron has a slope into it, a kink position, and a height. Start with the presets, then sculpt by hand: One ramp A bump (3 ramps) A wiggle N1 slope N1 kink at N1 height N2 slope N2 kink at N2 height N3 slope N3 kink at N3 height You are doing, by hand, exactly what training does automatically: choosing each neuron's slope, position, and contribution so the total curve takes a wanted shape. Training = a machine turning these same knobs, guided by gradients. Nothing else is new. From curves to boundaries The demo is one-dimensional for clarity, but the same story holds in 2-D and beyond: each hidden ReLU neuron contributes a crease along a line, and sums of creases sculpt curved decision boundaries — the bent borders that logistic regression, forever straight, could never dra

h: Try it: one neuron's entire personality
The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 4 · Neural networks The neuron: a weighted sum with an attitude Strip the mystique: a neuron is the linear recipe you already know, plus one bend. Everything "neural" is built from this. Before you start Logistic regression — because, surprise: you've already trained a neuron. The idea A neuron does exactly three things: Multiply each input by a weight (how much it matters). Add them up, plus a bias (the starting offset). Pass the total through a simple bending function — the activation function . Steps 1–2 are literally linear regression. Step 3 is the only new ingredient, and logistic regression already did a version of it (the sigmoid). So: a neuron is logistic regression wearing a biology costume. The brain metaphor ("fires when stimulation crosses a threshold") is where the name came from, but you lose nothing by thinking "weighted sum, then bend." Why the bend matters so much: if you stack pure weighted sums on top of each other, the stack collapses — a sum of sums is just another sum, so a hundred linear layers can only ever draw the same straight boundary as one. The activation's little nonlinearity is what stops the collapse and lets stacking buy new power. No bend, no deep learning. That's the whole secret, this early in the unit. Try it: one neuron's entire personality One input across the bottom, the neuron's output as the curve. The weight stretches and flips the response; the bias slides it; the activation decides its shape: Weight w (how strongly the input matters) 1.0 Bias b (slides the threshold) 0.0 Step (1958) Sigmoid Tanh ReLU Set weight negative and watch the curve flip — this neuron now votes against its input. Try ReLU with w = 2, b = −4: silent until the input passes 2, then increasingly loud. "Feature detectors" in big networks are exactly this: neurons that stay quiet until their particular pattern shows up. The math (open as much as you like) The idea in words Output = bend(weighted sum + offset). With many inputs the weighted sum is

h: The vocabulary you'll meet in the wild
b: Unit 4 · Neural networks Optimizers: how to spend a gradient Backprop tells you which way is downhill. It says nothing about how boldly to walk. That decision has fifty years of craft in it — and one race settles the intuition. Before you start Slopes & gradients and backpropagation . The idea Real loss landscapes are rarely nice round bowls. The classic villain is the narrow valley : steep walls across, a long gentle slope along. Plain gradient descent ( SGD ) suffers there, because the gradient mostly points at the far wall, not down the valley — so it zigzags wall-to-wall while inching along the floor. Two classic upgrades: Momentum : give the ball weight. Keep a running velocity; each gradient pushes it rather than dictating the step. Zigzag pushes alternate and cancel; the steady down-valley push accumulates. A heavy ball rolls through the chatter. Adam : give every knob its own step size. Track each parameter's typical gradient size and divide by it — loud directions get tamed, whisper-quiet ones get amplified. Roughly momentum + per-knob auto-scaling; it works well with little tuning, which is why it's the default in half the papers ever written. Try it: the race Same valley, same start, same learning rate. Shading shows the loss (darker = lower); the circle is the goal: Learning rate 0.40 Race! ● SGD: – ● Momentum: – ● Adam: – Run the race at learning rate 0.2, then 0.4, then 0.9. Watch SGD's zigzag get violent as the rate rises — at 0.9 it bounces between the walls. Momentum plows through; Adam takes an oddly straight, businesslike line because it rescales each direction separately. There is no single \"best\" — but you now know what each personality does, which is what the hyperparameter folklore is actually about. The vocabulary you'll meet in the wild The "stochastic" in SGD: real training computes each gradient on a small random mini-batch of examples, not the whole dataset — far cheaper per step, and the noise it adds is mostly harmless, sometimes even

h: Try it: whom does each word consult?
The math (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 5 · Deep learning Attention: every word looks around The mechanism inside the T of GPT. One idea — let each word gather information from the words that matter to it — reorganized the entire field. Before you start Embeddings and the dot product — attention is dot products between embeddings, plus bookkeeping. The problem The last page left a loose end: "bank" gets one embedding, whether river or savings. And meaning is relational in worse ways — in "the trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because it was too big" , the word "it" means trophy , and you know that only by consulting the rest of the sentence (swap "big" for "small" and "it" flips to the suitcase!). For years the standard fix was recurrent networks (RNNs, LSTMs): read left to right, carrying a running summary. It worked, but distant context faded — by word forty, word three was a rumor — and reading one-word-at-a-time made training painfully serial. Attention replaced the relay race with a conference call. The idea Attention lets every word, in one step, look directly at every other word and take a weighted average of what it finds. Per word, three moves: Ask. Each word broadcasts a query — "I'm a pronoun seeking my noun." Match. The query is compared (dot product — your old friend the agreement-meter) against every other word's advertisement of what it offers. Good matches score high. Blend. Scores become percentages, and the word updates itself with the percentage-weighted blend of the others' content. "It" comes out of the layer 60% flavored with "trophy" — effectively disambiguated . Every word does this simultaneously, and the whole thing stacks: a transformer is essentially attention layers alternating with small MLPs (Unit 4's networks!), repeated dozens of times. No recurrence, no fading with distance — word 1 reaches word 4,000 as easily as its neighbor — and everything computes in parallel, which is why transformers could be trained on internet-sized text. Try it: whom does each word consult?

h: Try it: slide the detector yourself
b: Unit 5 · Deep learning Convolutions: one small question, asked everywhere How networks see. The idea fits in a sentence: slide a tiny pattern-detector across the whole image and write down where it fires. Before you start The neuron — a convolution is that same weighted sum, on a leash. The idea Feeding an image to the playground's MLP would mean one weight per pixel per neuron — millions of knobs that would have to separately learn that a cat at the left of a photo is the same cat as at the right. Wasteful, and it ignores what images are like: the meaningful stuff is local (edges, corners, textures) and it can appear anywhere . The fix: make one tiny neuron — say 3×3 pixels' worth of weights, called a kernel (or filter) — and slide it across the whole image, computing its weighted sum at every position. The result is a new image showing where that little pattern occurs . This slide-and-sum is a convolution , and a network built from layers of them is a convolutional neural network (CNN). Two birds with one stone: the same 9 weights serve every position (a cat is a cat anywhere), and 9 weights instead of millions (the leash). Try it: slide the detector yourself A little scene, a 3×3 kernel, and the result. Hover the image to watch a single output pixel being computed — the window's 9 pixels times the kernel's 9 weights, added up. Then switch kernels: Do nothing Blur Sharpen Find edges Vertical lines only Horizontal lines only Compare "vertical lines only" and "horizontal lines only" on the house: same image, same machinery, 9 different numbers — and completely different features found. Now the punchline: in a CNN, nobody designs these kernels . They're weights. Backprop tunes them, and edge-detectors like these reliably emerge in the first layer on their own — because edges are what images are made of. Stacking the idea One convolution layer finds simple things (edges, spots of color). The next layer convolves over those results — so its patterns are patterns of edg

Unit 5 · Deep learning Embeddings: meaning as a place on a map Networks eat numbers, not words. The fix turned out to be one of the most beautiful ideas in the field: give every word a location, and let "similar meaning" become "nearby". Before you start Vectors — an embedding IS a vector; this page is that idea finally paying off in full. The idea How do you feed the word "cat" to a neural network? Numbering the dictionary (cat = 5,231) is poison: it implies cat is somehow "close to" word 5,230 and "far from" word 90,000, which is meaningless. What we want is numbers where distance means something . An embedding does exactly that: each word gets a list of, say, 300 numbers — its coordinates in a "meaning space." The coordinates are learned, not designed: train a network to predict a word from its surroundings across billions of sentences, and the only way it can succeed is by assigning similar coordinates to words used in similar ways. Meaning, as the residue of prediction. What emerges is eerie: directions in the space become concepts. There's a direction that means "royalty", one that means "plural", one that means gender — so arithmetic on coordinates does analogy: king − man + woman lands near queen. Try it: walk the map Twenty-six words with small hand-crafted meaning-vectors (six dimensions — real ones use hundreds), flattened onto your screen. Hover to see each word's true nearest neighbors; then do arithmetic on meaning below: hover a word… − + Try king − man + woman, then kitten − cat + dog, then king − crown + car (garbage in, garbage out — the space only contains what was put into it). Note the map's neighborhoods: royalty, animals, food, vehicles. Nobody drew those regions; they fall out of the vectors. Also honest fine print: this screen shows 2 of the 6 dimensions' worth of structure, so the flat picture slightly lies — the neighbor lists (computed in full 6-D) are the truth. h: Try it: the forward wrecking, and the walk back
The pieces you've already met, reassembled
b: Unit 6 · Modern ML Diffusion: images out of static The trick behind modern image generators sounds like a joke: teach a network to remove noise, then hand it pure noise and let it "remove" its way to a picture that never existed. Before you start Convolutions (the denoiser is a CNN-flavored network) and the general idea that networks learn by example. This is the dessert course — enjoy it. The idea Generating an image from scratch is monstrous: every pixel depends on every other. Diffusion breaks the monster into a thousand trivial steps, via a training scheme of ruthless cleverness: Wreck things (no learning needed). Take a real photo, add a little random static. Add a little more. Repeat until pure noise. Do this to millions of photos, keeping every intermediate stage. Learn to un-wreck slightly. Train a network on: given a partly-noised image (and how noisy it is), predict the noise that was added — so it can be subtracted back out. Just supervised learning! The "label" is the exact noise you yourself added one step ago. Data and labels, manufactured for free, in any quantity. Run it backwards to create. Start from pure random static and apply the denoiser step by step. Each step, it nudges the pixels toward "what a real image would look like if this were its noisy version." A thousand nudges later: a coherent picture — of nothing that ever existed, because the starting static was random. Try it: the forward wrecking, and the walk back A little landscape and a noise dial. The forward direction is the real training-data factory; the walk back illustrates generation: How much noise clean picture Dissolve into static Walk back to a picture Different static Find the dial position where the picture stops being recognizable to YOU (~60–75%?). A trained denoiser keeps recovering structure well past where your eye gives up — at every noise level, faint statistical echoes of the image survive, and it has seen millions of examples of exactly what they look like. h: The problem with a raw predictor
Prompting vs. fine-tuning: which lever when?
How do you grade a generated paragraph?
b: Unit 6 · Modern ML From predictor to assistant Pretraining builds a mind-bogglingly knowledgeable text predictor with no manners and no job description. Three add-on stages give it both. Before you start Large language models — this page is about what happens AFTER that machine exists. The problem with a raw predictor Ask a freshly pretrained model "How do I make bread?" and a perfectly correct continuation is "How do I make pasta? How do I make rice?" — because on the internet, questions travel in packs. The model isn't broken; it's answering the question it was actually trained on ("what text comes next?"), not the one you meant ("help me"). Closing that gap is called alignment , and it takes a tiny fraction of the compute that pretraining did — a finishing school, not a second education. The three stages Supervised fine-tuning . Continue training — same backprop, same log loss — on a small, curated set of example dialogues written the way an assistant should respond. Unit 2 vocabulary: it's just more training data, chosen to demonstrate the job. After this, the model answers questions instead of continuing them. Preference tuning ( RLHF and relatives). Some qualities are easier to recognize than to demonstrate: which of two answers is clearer? more honest? People rank pairs of model outputs; a reward model is trained to predict those rankings (a grader — a learned loss, really); then the assistant is tuned to score well on it. That's "reinforcement learning from human feedback": learning from grades instead of worked examples. A system prompt. The cheapest layer: standing instructions prepended invisibly to every conversation ("You are a helpful assistant. Decline harmful requests…"). No training at all — just context the predictor conditions on. Prompting vs. fine-tuning: which lever when? Once assistants existed, a new question appeared: to make the model do your task, do you change its input or its weights ? Prompting — change the input. Describe the task, sho

h: Try it: a language model that fits in your head
b: Unit 6 · Modern ML Large language models: next-word prediction, taken seriously Underneath the conversation is a machine doing one thing: given the text so far, score every possible next token. Everything else follows from doing that astonishingly well. Before you start Attention and embeddings — an LLM is those, stacked and scaled. First: what's a token? Models don't quite read words. Text is chopped into tokens — pieces from a fixed menu of a hundred thousand or so, learned by finding the most reusable fragments in a mountain of text. Common words get their own token; rarer words get built from parts (which is why a model can handle "unbreakableness" without ever having a token for it). This toy menu has ~30 pieces (real ones: ~100,000; same greedy idea). Type "walking talkers" — clean splits. Type "xylophone" — nothing on the menu matches, so it shatters into characters (red). Fun fact with real-world bite: this is why models are weirdly bad at counting letters in a word — they never see letters, only these chunks. The machine itself A large language model is a transformer trained on one task: given the tokens so far, predict the next one . Output: a probability for every token on the menu. Pick one (most-likely, or sample with some randomness — the dial called temperature), append it, repeat. Every chatbot reply you've ever read was generated one token at a time this way. Why does mere next-token prediction produce something that can explain, translate, and write code? Because predicting well forces learning. To continue "the capital of Finland is", you must know it. To continue a half-written function, you must model what the code does. Compress the internet into prediction weights and enormous amounts of world-knowledge, grammar, and reasoning-shaped patterns get dragged in — not because anyone asked for them, but because they reduce the loss (yes — the same log loss you met in logistic regression, summed over trillions of tokens). Two thousand actual handwritten digits, collected in the 1990s and classified by every ML student since — now it's your turn, live, in this tab. Before you start The playground . Same machinery here — the input just grew from 2 numbers to 784 (each digit is 28×28 pixels, every pixel a feature). Train it The network is [784 → hidden → 10]: pixels in, one score per digit out, softmax turning scores into percentages. Watch the rotating parade of test digits below — every digit is outlined by the model's current verdict (green = right, red = wrong, its guess in the corner): Hidden neurons Train the dataset loads when you press Train (~2 MB) steps: 0 · loss: – · accuracy on trained digits: – · on never-seen digits: – Now draw your own Draw a digit below (big, centered — like the samples above). The model classifies it as you draw. (2) Look at which red-boxed digits remain near the end: ambiguous 4s-vs-9s, 3s-vs-5s — the model's mistakes are largely the ones a tired human makes. (3) If your drawn digit gets misread, try drawing it bigger and centered: the model never saw off-center digits in training, so it has no idea what they are. Data is destiny, in your own handwriting. What just happened Nothing new — that's the point.
