Unit 8 · Expert track Build the autodiff engine yourself The rite of passage. You've used this site's autodiff engine since the backprop page — now write it from a blank file, and PyTorch stops being magic forever. Before you start Backpropagation , cold. Open your editor: this page is a guided build, and copying defeats it. Reference solution: assets/js/ml/autodiff.js — but only after each attempt. The contract One class, Value , wrapping one number, such that: const w = new Value(-0.5), x = new Value(2), b = new Value(0.4); const loss = w.mul(x).add(b).sigmoid().sub(1).pow(2); loss.backward(); console.log(w.grad); // exactly dLoss/dw — no numerical nudging Whatever expression the user builds, calling backward() on the result must fill every participant's .grad with its true nudge-effect on that result. That's the entire spec of an autodiff engine — the rest is deciding what bookkeeping makes it inevitable. The build, in five steps Step 1 — a Value remembers where it came from Try first: a class holding data (the number), grad (starts 0), and — the load-bearing part — for values produced by an operation, which values produced it . Every computation quietly builds a family tree. class Value { constructor(data, children = []) { this.data = data; this.grad = 0; this._children = children; this._backward = () => {}; // filled in by whoever creates me } } _backward is the design's one stroke of elegance: each operation will install a tiny closure that knows how to pass blame to its own parents. Nobody ever needs a global list of derivative rules — each Value carries its own. Step 2 — add and mul (write these before peeking) Each operation does three things: compute the result, record the parentage, install the blame-passer. The local derivatives you need: for addition, both parents inherit the child's gradient unchanged (d(a+b)/da = 1); for multiplication, each parent gets the child's gradient times the other parent's value (d(a·b)/da = b). add(other) { const out = new V

Unit 8 · Expert track How to read an ML paper Papers are written backwards, reviewed under deadline, and padded for prestige — and they're still where the field actually lives. Here's how practitioners extract the signal. Before you start Everything before this page IS the preparation: you can read the notation, you know the vocabulary, and you've trained the models papers take as given. The shape of the beast Nearly every ML paper is the same eight parts: title/abstract (the sales pitch), introduction (the sales pitch, longer), related work (politics and genealogy), method (the actual idea, in armor-plated notation), experiments (the evidence), ablations (which parts of the idea actually matter — often the most honest section), limitations (increasingly real, historically decorative), appendix (where the reproducibility details hide). The single most useful fact about this genre: the idea is usually one sentence , and the paper is thirty pages of armor around it. Attention's paper is "let every position query every other directly." Diffusion's is "learn to undo noise, then start from pure noise." Your job as a reader is to find that sentence, not to absorb the armor. Three passes, not one Pass 1 — five minutes, no honor at stake. Title, abstract, all figures and their captions, conclusion. ML papers put their soul in Figure 1 far more often than in Section 3. Exit question: what's the one-sentence idea, and do I care? Pass 2 — thirty minutes, pencil out. Read for structure, skipping proofs and derivations. For every equation, do what this site trained you to do: find the verb, name each symbol aloud, translate to a plain sentence — most fearsome equations are "weighted average" or "our loss has two terms" in armor. Look hard at the experiments' baselines : what is this beating, and is that comparison fair (same data? same compute? tuned equally hard)? Pass 3 — hours, only for the few that earn it. Re-derive, or better: re-implement small. You've already done this o

h: 1. The model learned the wrong thing perfectly
2. Leakage: the answer was hiding in the inputs
5. Goodhart's law: the metric ate the goal
b: Unit 8 · Expert track When ML fails: the expert's real syllabus The math on this site mostly works. Deployed systems mostly fail anyway — and almost never for mathematical reasons. This page is the difference between knowing ML and being trusted with it. Before you start Unit 2 — every failure here is one of its lessons, escaped into the world and grown teeth. The recurring villains 1. The model learned the wrong thing perfectly A famous genre: the tank detector that actually detected sunny weather (all tank photos were taken on sunny days); the pneumonia model that learned to read which hospital's X-ray machine took the image (sicker patients went to sicker hospitals); the wolf classifier that was a snow classifier. Models optimize the loss on the data given — they have no loyalty whatsoever to the concept in your head. This is called shortcut learning , and its detector is Unit 2 honesty applied ruthlessly: test on data where the shortcut breaks (cloudy tanks, same machine, wolves on grass) before someone's safety depends on it. 2. Leakage: the answer was hiding in the inputs The model predicting patient death with uncanny accuracy — using features that included "palliative care ordered." True on the training data, useless in the clinic (by then you don't need a model), and worse than useless before (the signal isn't there yet). Leakage is any feature that won't exist, or won't mean the same thing, at prediction time. It's the single most common cause of "too good to be true" results — so treat too-good-to-be-true as a smoke alarm, not a triumph. 3. The world moved: distribution shift Every model is a photograph of its training data's era. Consumer behavior models trained on 2019 met 2020. Spam evolves against the filter (the adversary reads your boundary and routes around it). Nothing in training warns you — the loss was minimized on a world that no longer exists. Deployed ML is therefore a process : monitor live accuracy, watch input distributions drift, retrain

Getting a computer to work out the rule from examples, instead of being handed the rule. A family of methods where a program improves at a task by adjusting itself based on data, rather than following instructions a person wrote for every case. "Learning" means: change the settings until the outputs match the examples better.

The rule-of-thumb machine we're building — settings plus a recipe that turns an input into a prediction. A mathematical function with adjustable settings (parameters) that maps inputs to outputs. A straight line with its slope and offset is a model; so is a billion-parameter neural network. "Training a model" means tuning its parameters.

The guess-grade-adjust loop: try settings, measure how wrong the guesses are, nudge the settings, repeat. The process of adjusting a model's parameters to reduce its loss on a set of examples. Most commonly done with gradient descent.

One example: one row in your notebook, one dot on the chart. A single recorded case, usually an input (or several) together with the answer we'd like the model to produce for it. A dataset is a collection of data points.

One of the things we know about each example — a column in the notebook, like the day's temperature. An individual measurable property used as input to a model. Real problems usually have many features per data point; choosing and preparing them well (feature engineering) often matters more than the choice of model.

The right answer for an example — what actually happened, like that day's real sales. The known output value attached to a data point during training. The model's predictions are graded against the labels.
The model's guess for an input it's given. The output a model produces for a given input. Written ŷ ("y-hat") to distinguish the guess from the true answer y.

A setting inside the model that training is allowed to change — a knob the learning turns. A value the training process adjusts to fit the data, such as a line's slope and offset, or a neural network's millions of weights. Distinct from hyperparameters, which are set by the human before training starts.

How much an input matters to the prediction. For a line on a chart, it's the slope. A parameter that multiplies an input. A large weight means that input strongly influences the output; zero means it's ignored; negative means it pushes the output the other way.

The model's starting offset: the prediction when every input is zero. For a line, where it crosses the axis. A parameter added to the weighted inputs, letting the model's output start somewhere other than zero. (Unrelated to "bias" meaning unfairness in data — that's a different concept that shares the word.)

The model's grade: how wrong its guesses are, boiled down to one number. Lower is better, like golf. A function that measures the gap between predictions and true answers across the data. Training is the search for parameter values that make the loss small. Different tasks use different losses; mean squared error is the classic one for regression.

Grade a model by taking each miss, squaring it, and averaging: misses in both directions count, and big misses count extra. A loss for regression: the average of (true value − prediction)² over all data points. Squaring keeps misses above and below from cancelling out and punishes large errors disproportionately — which is also why outliers pull hard on the fit.

One example's miss: the gap between the real answer and the model's guess — the little vertical bar from a dot down to the line. The difference y − ŷ for a single data point. Patterns in the residuals (rather than random scatter) are a sign the model is missing structure in the data.

A weird dot far from all the others — a typo, a fluke, or a genuinely unusual case. A data point that lies unusually far from the overall pattern. Squared-error losses give outliers outsized influence, so handling them (investigating, capping, or using a robust loss) is a routine part of real ML work.

The "which way is uphill" arrow: for each setting, does the loss go up or down if I nudge it, and how steeply? The collection of a function's derivatives with respect to each parameter — the direction of steepest increase. Training moves parameters against the gradient (downhill) to shrink the loss.

How fast one thing changes when you nudge another — the mathematical answer to "if I turn this knob a tiny bit, what happens?" The rate of change of a function at a point. The partial derivative ∂L/∂w reads "how the loss L changes as the weight w changes, holding everything else still." It is the core tool that lets training know which way to adjust each parameter.

Learning by walking downhill: repeatedly nudge every setting in whichever direction makes the grade a little better. An algorithm that iteratively updates parameters a small step against the gradient of the loss: w ← w − η·∂L/∂w. Almost all of deep learning is powered by variants of this loop (SGD, momentum, Adam).

How big each nudge is. Too small and learning crawls; too big and it overshoots and bounces around. A hyperparameter multiplying the gradient in each update step. One of the first knobs to tune when training misbehaves.

Fitting the best straight line through your dots, then using the line to predict. A model whose prediction is a weighted sum of the inputs plus a bias: ŷ = wx + b (one input) or ŷ = w₁x₁ + … + wₙxₙ + b (many). Usually fit by minimizing mean squared error — which has a closed-form solution, but is also the standard first example of gradient descent.

Predicting a number: tomorrow's temperature, a house price, how many ice creams. The family of tasks where the output is a continuous quantity. Contrast with classification, where the output is a category.

Predicting a category: spam or not, cat or dog, which digit is in the picture. The family of tasks where the output is one of a fixed set of classes. Uses different losses and metrics than regression (accuracy, precision/recall, cross-entropy).

Learning from worked examples: every practice question comes with the right answer attached. Training on data points that include labels, so the model's guesses can be graded directly. Regression and classification are both supervised.

Learning without answer keys: find structure in the data on your own, like sorting a pile of photos into groups. Methods that work on unlabeled data — clustering (k-means), dimensionality reduction (PCA), density estimation. The model discovers patterns rather than matching known answers.
The examples the model practices on — the homework. The portion of the data used to fit the parameters. Performance here is not proof of learning: a model can ace data it memorized.

Examples held back and never shown during practice — the real exam. Data kept aside to estimate how the model performs on cases it hasn't seen. Looking at it during development erodes its value; that's what a separate validation set is for.

Memorizing the homework instead of learning the subject: perfect on practice examples, lost on new ones. When a model captures noise and quirks of the training set rather than the underlying pattern, so training loss keeps improving while test performance gets worse. The central occupational disease of ML; regularization, more data, and simpler models are the treatments.

The model is too simple to catch the pattern at all — a straight line through a clearly curved trend. When a model lacks the capacity (or training) to capture the real structure, performing poorly even on the training set. The cure is a richer model, better features, or longer training.

A setting the human picks before training starts — training tunes the parameters, you tune the hyperparameters. Configuration values not learned from data: learning rate, model size, number of training steps, regularization strength. Chosen by experiment, usually judged on a validation set.

Many simple weighted-sum units stacked in layers, with gentle bends between them — enough to learn shapes no straight line can. A model built from layers of linear transformations (weights and biases) separated by nonlinear activation functions. Trained end-to-end with gradient descent via backpropagation. The foundation of deep learning.

A list of numbers — which is also an arrow: [3, 1] is "walk 3 east, 1 north." An ordered list of numbers, viewable geometrically as a point or arrow in space with one dimension per entry. In ML, every data point is a vector (one entry per feature), and so are a model's parameters and gradients.

A table of numbers — or equally, a stack of vectors, one row per data point. A rectangular grid of numbers with rows and columns. A dataset of n examples with d features is an n×d matrix; a neural network layer's weights form a matrix that transforms one vector into another.

Multiply two lists position by position and add it all up: one number that measures how much two directions agree. For equal-length vectors a and b: Σᵢ aᵢbᵢ. Positive when they point similar ways, zero at right angles, negative when opposed. The core operation inside linear models and every neural network layer.
The plain average: add everything up, divide by how many. The sum of values divided by their count; the center of mass of a distribution. Written μ for a population or x̄ ("x-bar") for a sample.

The average of the squared gaps from the mean — spread, measured in squared units. σ²: the mean of (value − mean)² over the data. Its square root is the standard deviation, which lives back in ordinary units. Note the family resemblance to mean squared error — MSE is the variance of the misses.

How far values typically stray from their average — tight huddle or wide scatter, as one number. The square root of the variance. For roughly bell-shaped data, ~68% of values fall within one σ of the mean and ~95% within two.

The shape of a pile of values: where they cluster, how far they spread, whether they lean. A description of how likely each possible value is. Summarized by statistics like the mean and standard deviation; visualized as a histogram or density curve.

The bell curve: values pile up symmetrically around the average and thin out fast on both sides. The distribution that arises whenever many small independent effects add up — which is why measurement noise so often follows it. Fully described by just μ and σ. The 68/95 rule of thumb comes from its shape.

A middle pile of data for repeated tune-and-check cycles — so the real test set stays untouched until the very end. Data held out from training and used to compare model variants and tune hyperparameters. Because you iterate against it, it slowly gets "used up" too — which is why a final, once-only test set is kept separate.

Doing well on new examples, not just the ones you practiced on. The entire point of learning. A model's performance on data drawn from the same source but not seen during training. Estimated by the test-set grade; the train-vs-test gap measures how much of the training performance was memorization.

A fee for wildness: penalize dramatic settings so the model only bends where the data genuinely pays for it. Any technique that discourages overfitting by constraining a model's effective flexibility — most classically by adding a penalty term (scaled by λ) on parameter size to the loss. Weight decay, dropout, and early stopping are all regularizers in spirit.

Where you draw the line on a model's score: above → flag it, below → let it pass. The score above which a classifier's output is treated as positive. Moving it trades false alarms against misses (precision against recall); the right setting depends on which mistake costs more, not on the model.

The share of cases handled correctly — and nearly meaningless when the thing you're hunting is rare. (correct predictions) / (all predictions). With imbalanced classes it mostly reflects the imbalance: "never fraud" is 99.9% accurate if fraud is 1-in-1,000. Always read it alongside precision and recall.

When you point, are you right? Of everything flagged, the share that truly was the thing. TP / (TP + FP): among positive predictions, the fraction that are actually positive. Low precision means crying wolf. Raise the threshold and precision typically rises — at recall's expense.

Of what's really out there, how much did you find? The share of actual cases you caught. TP / (TP + FN): among actual positives, the fraction the model flagged. Low recall means wolves stroll past. The king metric whenever a miss is the catastrophic error (screening, fraud, safety).

A false alarm: the model flagged something that was actually fine. A negative case predicted as positive — a real email junked, a healthy patient flagged. Its cost relative to the false negative's should drive where you set the threshold.

A miss: the thing was really there, and the model let it slip past. A positive case predicted as negative — spam reaching the inbox, a disease left undetected. When this is the catastrophic error, optimize for recall and accept more false alarms.

The 2×2 scorecard: caught, correctly passed, false alarms, and misses — all four counts visible at once. A table of prediction vs. reality counts (TP, FP, TN, FN for two classes; N×N for more). Every headline metric — accuracy, precision, recall — is a ratio of cells from this table, which is why it's the honest place to start.

Linear regression's yes/no cousin: score with the weighted sum, then squash the score into a probability. A classifier computing σ(w·x + b): a linear score passed through the sigmoid, trained with log loss. Its decision boundary is a straight line (hyperplane). Despite the name, it does classification — still the standard first model for yes/no problems.

The S-shaped squasher: any number in, something between 0 and 1 out — big scores land near 1, big negatives near 0. σ(z) = 1/(1+e⁻ᶻ). Maps the real line smoothly onto (0, 1) with σ(0) = 0.5, turning raw scores into probabilities. Also one of the classic activation functions in neural networks.

The line where a classifier changes its mind — 50/50 territory between "yes" and "no". The surface in feature-space where a classifier's predicted class flips. Linear models draw straight boundaries; trees draw axis-aligned boxes; kNN and neural networks can draw arbitrarily wiggly ones. A model's boundary shape IS its flexibility.

A grade for probability-speakers: pay by how little probability you gave the truth — confident wrongness costs dearly. For true label y and predicted probability p̂: −[y·log(p̂) + (1−y)·log(1−p̂)], averaged over examples. Punishes overconfident mistakes hard, which trains honest uncertainty. The classification counterpart of mean squared error, and the loss behind most of deep learning.

Classify by asking the k most similar known examples to vote. No training — the dataset is the model. A lazy learner: store all data; at query time, find the k closest points by distance and take the majority label (or average, for regression). Sensitive to feature scaling and slow at query time on big data — but simple, strong, and a perfect intuition pump.

A flowchart of yes/no questions about the features, grown automatically — the rare model you can read. A model that recursively splits data with single-feature threshold questions, choosing each split to maximize class purity (e.g. minimize Gini impurity). Fully interpretable, trains fast, and overfits enthusiastically when grown deep — which is why it's usually deployed inside a forest.

Many deep trees, each trained on a random re-sample of the data, voting together — their private mistakes cancel, the shared pattern survives. An ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrap samples (and typically random feature subsets), predictions combined by majority vote or averaging. Trades the single tree's readability for markedly better generalization; a perennial best-first-try on tabular data.

A committee of models. Individually shaky, collectively steady — because their errors disagree and average away. Any method combining multiple models' predictions (voting, averaging, stacking, boosting). Works exactly to the degree the members' errors are decorrelated — hence the deliberate randomness in how members are trained.

Finding natural groups in data that nobody labeled — sorting the pile without being told the categories. The core unsupervised task: partition data points so members of a group are similar and groups are distinct. k-means is the classic algorithm; success is judged by group tightness/separation and usefulness, since no ground truth exists.

Pick k guess-centers, then repeat two steps: every dot joins its nearest center; every center moves to the middle of its dots. A clustering algorithm minimizing total squared dot-to-center distance by alternating assignment and centroid-update steps until stable. Always converges, but to a local optimum — run several random restarts and keep the tightest. k is chosen by the human.

A group's middle point: the plain average of its members, feature by feature. The component-wise mean of a set of vectors — the point minimizing total squared distance to the set, which is precisely why k-means' update step moves each center there.

A weighted sum with an attitude: weigh the inputs, add a bias, bend the result. The basic network component: output = g(w·x + b), where g is an activation function. Logistic regression is exactly one neuron with a sigmoid bend; networks are many of them wired in layers.

The bend applied after a neuron's weighted sum — without it, stacked layers collapse into one. A simple nonlinear function (ReLU, tanh, sigmoid…) applied to each neuron's pre-activation. Since compositions of linear maps are linear, this is the ingredient that makes depth add expressive power.

"Off below zero, a plain ramp above." Crude, cheap, and the workhorse bend of modern deep learning. ReLU(z) = max(0, z). Its slope is exactly 1 wherever it's on — no fading gradients at the extremes like the S-curves — which is much of why deep networks became trainable. Each ReLU neuron contributes one crease to the network's function.

Any layer between input and output. "Hidden" just means nobody directly observes or grades its outputs. A layer of neurons whose activations are intermediate computation, not data or answer. Width (neurons per layer) and depth (number of layers) are the main architecture dials — both are bendiness dials in the Unit 2 sense.

The plain, bread-and-butter neural network: layers of neurons, each layer feeding the next. A stack of fully-connected layers with activations between, trained by backprop. The universal-approximation result says a wide enough MLP can represent nearly any function — finding those weights, and justifying them with data, is the actual work.

Blame, passed backwards: run the network forward saving every value, then walk the recipe in reverse, multiplying local slopes, until every weight knows its nudge-effect. The chain rule organized for efficiency: all gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter in one forward + one backward pass — versus one full pass per parameter for naive nudging. The algorithm that makes training large networks feasible.

Nudge-effects along a chain multiply: if w moves z by 2× and z moves the loss by 3×, then w moves the loss by 6×. d(f∘g)/dx = f′(g(x))·g′(x). The single calculus fact underneath backpropagation — applied step by step from the loss backwards through every operation in the network.

Plain gradient descent, fed by small random batches of data instead of the whole dataset each step. Gradient descent where each step's gradient is estimated on a mini-batch. Cheap per step and noisy in a mostly-harmless way. The baseline optimizer; momentum and Adam are upgrades on top of it.

Give the rolling ball weight: keep a running velocity so zigzag pushes cancel and steady pushes compound. An optimizer keeping an exponentially-decaying average of past gradients as a velocity (v ← βv − η∇L; θ ← θ + v). Damps oscillation across narrow valleys and accelerates along them.

Momentum plus a personal step size for every knob — loud directions tamed, quiet ones amplified. The works-out-of-the-box default. An optimizer tracking per-parameter running means of gradients (momentum) and squared gradients (scale), taking steps of the ratio. Robust to hyperparameter choices, hence its ubiquity.

A small random handful of examples used to estimate the gradient for one training step. The per-step data sample in SGD (commonly 32–4096 examples). Bigger batches: smoother gradients, more compute per step. The noise from small batches is a mild, sometimes beneficial regularizer.

One full lap through the training data. One pass over every training example. Training runs are measured in epochs or steps; watching validation loss across epochs is how early stopping decides when learning has turned into memorizing.

Slide a tiny pattern-detector across the whole image, computing its weighted sum at every position — the result shows where the pattern occurs. An operation applying the same small kernel of weights at every spatial position of its input, producing a feature map of responses. Weight sharing encodes "this pattern means the same thing anywhere" and drastically cuts parameter count.

The tiny grid of weights that gets slid across an image — a 3×3 kernel is a nine-number question asked at every position. The shared weight patch of a convolution. In a trained CNN kernels are learned parameters; early layers reliably develop edge- and texture-detectors because those patterns are the atoms of natural images.

A network built from stacked convolution layers: edges → corners → parts → objects, each layer detecting patterns of the previous layer's patterns. A network alternating convolution layers, nonlinearities, and periodic downsampling (pooling), trained end-to-end by backprop. The default architecture for images for a decade, and still the local-structure workhorse inside many modern systems.

A learned list of coordinates for a discrete thing (word, product, user) — placed so that similar things sit nearby. A dense learned vector representing a discrete item, trained so geometric relationships (distance, direction) track functional similarity. The input layer of every language model, and the machinery behind semantic search and recommendations.

Every word looks at every other word and takes a weighted blend of what it finds — weights computed fresh from the actual sentence. A mechanism where each position emits a query, scores it against every position's key (scaled dot products through softmax), and aggregates their values by those weights. Gives direct any-to-any information flow with full parallelism — the core of the transformer.

The architecture of modern AI: attention layers (words consult each other) alternating with small MLPs (each word thinks), stacked deep. A network of repeated blocks — multi-head self-attention followed by per-position feed-forward layers, with residual connections and normalization — over token embeddings. Replaced recurrence for sequences because it trains in parallel and handles long-range dependencies directly.

The standard machine for turning any list of scores into positive percentages that add to 100% — big scores rewarded disproportionately. softmax(z)ᵢ = e^{zᵢ} / Σⱼ e^{zⱼ}. Converts raw scores into a probability distribution; used for attention weights, classifier outputs, and next-token probabilities alike. Its "temperature" variant divides scores first, controlling how peaked the result is.

One piece from the model's fixed menu of text fragments — common words are one token, rare words get built from parts. The atomic unit of text for a language model, produced by a learned subword vocabulary (~50k–200k entries, e.g. BPE). Models see only token IDs — which explains characteristic quirks like poor letter-counting.

A transformer trained on one task at planetary scale: given the tokens so far, predict the next one. Everything else is a side effect done well. A large transformer pretrained with next-token prediction (log loss) on vast text corpora, then typically aligned via fine-tuning and preference optimization. Generates by repeatedly scoring the vocabulary and appending a chosen token.

The fixed number of recent tokens the model can see. Outside it, nothing exists — and between conversations, nothing persists. The maximum sequence length a transformer processes at once. Attention's cost grows with window size, making long contexts an active engineering frontier. The model's only "memory" absent external tooling.

The big, expensive first phase: learn next-token prediction from a mountain of text, absorbing knowledge as a side effect. Self-supervised training on broad unlabeled data before any task-specific work. Produces a general "foundation" model with capability but no job description — alignment stages supply that afterwards, at a tiny fraction of the cost.

Continue training an existing model on a small curated dataset — same backprop, new specialty or manners. Further gradient training of a pretrained model on task- or behavior-specific examples. Supervised fine-tuning on example dialogues is what turns a raw predictor into an assistant; the Unit 2 overfitting risks apply in miniature.

Steer the model by changing its input, not its weights: describe the task, show examples, paste in documents. Zero training, instant iteration. Task specification via the model's context: instructions, demonstrations ("few-shot"), and reference material. A strong prompt often matches what previously needed a training run; the always-try-first lever. Retrieval-augmented prompting adds fetched documents per query.

Teach by grading instead of demonstrating: people rank pairs of answers, a grader-model learns those tastes, the assistant trains to score well on it. Alignment via a reward model trained on human preference comparisons, then policy optimization of the LLM against it. Exploits the asymmetry that recognizing quality is easier than producing it; relatives (DPO etc.) reach similar ends more directly.

Learn to remove a little noise from images; then start from pure static and "remove" your way, step by step, to a picture that never existed. A generative model trained to predict the noise added to data at every corruption level (free supervised labels), then sampled by iteratively denoising random noise. Text conditioning enters via embeddings at each step. The engine of modern image generation.

A model whose job is to produce new examples that look like the data — text, images, audio — rather than to label existing ones. A model of the data distribution itself, sampled to create novel instances. LLMs (sequential) and diffusion models (iterative refinement) are today's dominant families. Evaluation is intrinsically harder than for classifiers — there is no single right answer to compare against.
say AL-fah. A small tuning number — often a learning rate or a mixing weight.
say BAY-tah. Another tuning number; also the weights in classical statistics, and momentum's decay factor.
say DEL-tah. "The change in" — Δx is "how much x changed." Lowercase δ often marks a small error signal (backprop's per-neuron blame).
say EP-sih-lon. A tiny amount: leftover noise, or a small constant added to avoid dividing by zero.
say AY-tah. The learning rate — how big each training nudge is.
say THAY-tah. "All the model's settings, bundled into one name." When you see θ, think "the knobs."
say LAM-dah. A strength dial — classically, how hard regularization punishes dramatic settings.
say MEW. An average. μ with a subscript (μ_c) is the average of one group — a cluster center.

h: little o with a hat handle
b: say SIG-mah. Two jobs, context decides: the standard deviation (spread around the average), OR the sigmoid squashing function σ(z).
say SIG-mah. "Sum of": add up what follows, once per item in the range shown below and above it.
say PIE. 3.14159…, and in reinforcement learning, "a policy" — a strategy for choosing actions.

h: circle with a stick through it
b: say FIE (or FEE). Conventionally, a transformation applied to inputs — "the features, after processing."
say is. The sentence's verb. Split the equation here: left = the thing being defined, right = the recipe.
say is roughly. Equal for practical purposes — often exact in the limit as some nudge shrinks to nothing.
say becomes. An update: replace the left side with the result on the right. The = of code, in math clothes.
say the sum of. Add up what follows, once per value of the counter. A for-loop in a toga.
say the product of. Same as ∑, but multiply instead of add.
say is in. Membership: "x ∈ D" reads "x is one of the items in D."
say the absolute value of x. Drop the minus sign: how big, ignoring direction.
say the norm of x. The length of a vector (an arrow). ‖a − b‖ is the distance between two points.
say x-hat. "Our estimate of x" — the hat marks a guess. ŷ is the model's prediction of the true y.
say x sub i. Item number i in a list. The subscript is a counter, nothing more.
say x squared. Repeated multiplication: x·x. Squaring makes everything positive and punishes big values extra — why it's inside so many losses.
say the square root of. Undo a squaring: √9 = 3. Often used to return to ordinary units after squaring gaps.
say partial. "How this changes as that changes," nudging one input while the others hold still. ∂L/∂w = the loss's sensitivity to one weight.
say nabla (or "grad"). All the partial derivatives at once, bundled into one vector. Points uphill; training steps the other way.
say the expected value of. The long-run average of something random, weighted by how likely each outcome is.
say arg-min. "The setting that makes the following smallest (largest)" — not the small value itself, but WHICH knob position achieves it. Training is one giant argmin of the loss.
say log. The undo of e-to-the-power. In losses: gentle near 1 (small penalty for near-certainty in the truth), brutal near 0 (huge penalty for confident wrongness).
say ee. ≈ 2.718, the constant nature keeps choosing. e⁻ᶻ shrinks fast as z grows — the engine inside sigmoid and softmax.
say ex. What we predict from. Bold 𝐱 = the whole input vector (all features).
say why. The label: what actually happened. The model's guesses are graded against it.
say why-hat. The model's guess at y. Hat = estimate.
say weight. How much an input matters. Bold = one weight per feature; 𝐰·𝐱 is the dot product at the heart of nearly every model.
say bee. The starting offset — the prediction when every input is zero. (Unrelated to dataset bias; the word is overworked.)
say ell. The model's grade: how wrong it is, as one number. Lower is better. Also seen as J (an older convention for the same idea).
say en. How many: data points, features, whatever's being counted. Dividing by n turns totals into averages.
say SIG-moid of z. The S-shaped squasher: any score in, a 0-to-1 probability out.
say soft-max. Scores in, percentages out (positive, summing to 100%). Sigmoid's many-option sibling: attention weights, classifiers, next-token probabilities.

t: q, k, v — query, key, value
b: say query, key, value. Attention's three roles: what I'm looking for, what each word advertises, what each word actually hands over.
say the distance between a and b. How far apart two data points are — usually the straight-line (Euclidean) distance.
say gee of z. The bend after a neuron's weighted sum: ReLU, tanh, sigmoid. Without it, stacked layers collapse into one.
